era_context: The early 15th century continues with the Ming Dynasty under the Xuande Emperor (r. 1426–1435), the Ottoman Empire consolidating power, the Italian Renaissance in its early flowering, and the Hussite Wars ongoing in Central Europe. The Council of Constance is in its final years. Portuguese exploration of the African coast is beginning. The Aztec Empire is consolidating control over central Mexico.

events.title: Joan of Arc captured by Burgundian forces; beginning of her captivity
events.description: Joan of Arc, the French peasant girl who claimed divine inspiration and led French military campaigns during the Hundred Years' War, is captured by Burgundian forces at Compiègne on 23 May 1430. Joan had been leading French military campaigns since 1429, achieving significant victories, including the relief of Orléans and the coronation of Charles VII at Reims. Her capture marks a turning point: she is handed over to the English, who arrange for her trial for heresy and witchcraft. Joan's captivity lasts approximately one year; she is executed by burning in Rouen on 30 May 1431. Her capture and subsequent execution become defining events of the Hundred Years' War and have profound symbolic significance for French nationalism and religious martyrdom.
key_figures: Joan of Arc (French military leader, captured)
Charles VII (King of France, unable to ransom Joan)
Henry VI (King of England, beneficiary of Joan's capture)

sources.name: Trial records of Joan of Arc (compiled after her capture)
Jean de Metz and Jean de Belleville, contemporary accounts

events.certainty_note: Joan of Arc's capture is well-documented in contemporary chronicles and trial records. The date (23 May 1430) is firmly established.

events.title: Council of Constance: final sessions and ecclesiastical reform
events.description: The Council of Constance, which began in 1414, continues its final sessions in 1430. The council has already condemned Jan Hus (1415) and Jerome of Prague (1416) for heresy and has worked to heal the Western Schism. In 1430, the council is addressing remaining ecclesiastical reform issues and preparing for its formal conclusion in 1434. The council's work represents a major reassertion of papal authority and an attempt to address corruption and schism within the Catholic Church.

sources.name: Ulrich of Richenthal, Chronik des Konstanzer Konzils (contemporary chronicle)

events.certainty_note: The Council of Constance's sessions are extensively documented. The 1430 date is confirmed in official records.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet: fourth major voyage returns
events.description: Zheng He's treasure fleet returns from its fourth major voyage (1430–1433), which explored the Indian Ocean, reaching East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian subcontinent. The fleet comprises hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of personnel. The voyage demonstrates Ming China's technological superiority in naval construction and navigation. However, the expeditions are increasingly opposed by Confucian officials at court, who view them as wasteful. This voyage will be followed by a fifth voyage (1431–1433), a sixth voyage (1433–1435), and a seventh and final voyage (1433–1434), after which the Ming court will cease to fund large-scale maritime expeditions.
key_figures: Xuande Emperor (reigning 1426–1435, patron of the voyage)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records), official court records
Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan (The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: The dates of Zheng He's voyages are documented in Ming court records, but the exact return dates of individual voyages are sometimes uncertain. The fourth voyage is dated to 1430–1433 in official sources, but the return date may be 1430 or 1431.

events.title: Hussite Wars: Taborite military campaigns continue
events.description: The Hussite Wars continue in 1430, with the radical Taborite faction conducting military campaigns against Catholic forces and moderate Hussite opponents. The Taborites, inspired by the legacy of Jan Hus and John Žižka, advocate radical social and religious reform. In 1430, the Taborites are at the height of their military power, though they face increasing opposition from a coalition of moderate Hussites (Utraquists) and Catholic forces. The year 1430 represents the final phase of radical Hussite dominance; the Battle of Lipany in 1434 will decisively defeat the Taborites.

sources.name: Laurentius of Březová, Hussite Chronicle (contemporary Hussite account)

events.certainty_note: The Hussite Wars are well-documented, but specific military campaigns within 1430 are not always precisely dated. The general context of Taborite military activity is confirmed.

events.title: Italian Renaissance: Donatello's sculptural innovations in Florence
events.description: Donatello (Donato di Niccolò di Betto Bardi), one of the greatest sculptors of the Renaissance, is active in Florence in 1430. Donatello is revolutionizing sculpture by introducing classical principles of proportion, anatomy, and perspective. In 1430, Donatello is working on major commissions, including sculptures for the Florence Cathedral and the Basilica of Santa Croce. His work represents a break from medieval sculptural traditions and a return to classical principles. Donatello's innovations in sculpture parallel Brunelleschi's innovations in architecture and Masaccio's innovations in painting, making Florence the center of Renaissance artistic innovation. Donatello's work will influence sculptors throughout Europe for centuries.
key_figures: Cosimo de' Medici (patron, though exiled in 1433)

sources.name: Giorgio Vasari, Lives of the Artists (written in the 16th century, based on earlier sources)
Archaeological and art historical evidence from surviving sculptures

events.certainty_note: Donatello's activity in Florence in 1430 is documented in contemporary records and later biographical sources. However, the exact dates of specific sculptures are sometimes uncertain, and attributions are occasionally disputed by art historians.

disconfirming_evidence: Joan of Arc's capture is sometimes presented as a sudden reversal of fortune, but modern scholarship emphasizes that her military campaigns had already begun to face setbacks before her capture. The claim that she was betrayed by Charles VII is disputed; Charles was unable to ransom her due to political and financial constraints, not because he abandoned her. The significance of Joan's capture and execution in determining the outcome of the Hundred Years' War is sometimes exaggerated; the war continued for another 23 years after her death, and the French victory was ultimately determined by broader political and military factors. The Council of Constance's work in 1430 is sometimes presented as nearly complete, but the council continued to address significant issues until its formal conclusion in 1434.
historiographic_note: The year 1430 is well-documented for Europe, particularly France and Central Europe, due to abundant chronicle sources and administrative records. Joan of Arc's capture is one of the most famous events of the medieval period and is extensively documented. Ming China is also well-documented through official court records. However, sources for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are sparse or absent. The historical record for 1430 is heavily weighted toward Europe and East Asia.

graph_edges.note: Joan of Arc's capture in 1430 marks the end of her military campaigns, which began in 1429.
Joan of Arc's capture in 1430 leads to her trial and execution in 1431.
The Council of Constance's work in 1430 leads to its formal conclusion in 1434.
Zheng He's fourth voyage (1430–1433) is part of the series of treasure fleet expeditions that culminate in his final voyage in 1434.
Hussite military campaigns in 1430 lead to the decisive Battle of Lipany in 1434.
Donatello's sculptural innovations in 1430 are contemporary with Brunelleschi's architectural innovations, both contributing to the early Renaissance in Florence.
